stages.label: Dynamics of Singularity
stages.description: What happens with AI capability, and who has access?

stages.label: Dynamics of Control
stages.description: Is AI aligned? Who holds power, and what do they do with it?

stages.label: Rate of Impact
stages.description: How fast does the transformation reach humanity?

questions.text: Do you think AI will continue to double in task-completion ability every ~7 months?
questions.context: In March 2025, METR published research showing that the length of tasks AI agents can reliably complete has been doubling approximately every 7 months for the past 6 years. At that time, frontier models like Claude 3.7 Sonnet could reliably handle autonomous tasks taking humans about an hour. If the trend holds, AI agents would handle week-long tasks by 2027 and month-long projects by the end of the decade.

source.label: METR — Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks (March 2025)

answers.label: Yes — the trend continues
answers.description: Scaling laws hold, compute keeps growing, and AI capability maintains its exponential trajectory.

answers.label: No — it slows down or hits a wall
answers.description: Something breaks the trend: diminishing returns on scaling, data limitations, architectural ceilings, or something unexpected.

questions.text: Where do you think autonomous AI capability tops out?
questions.context: If the exponential trend doesn't hold, AI will hit a ceiling somewhere. The question is where. Think not about what AI can do with constant human guidance, but what it can handle reliably on its own — start to finish, no hand-holding.

answers.label: Around where we are now — hours at best
answers.description: AI stays great at short tasks but never reliably handles longer autonomous work. The copilot era is the final era.

answers.label: Day-level tasks
answers.description: AI can reliably handle a full day's worth of autonomous work, but falls apart on anything requiring multi-day coherence.

answers.label: Week-level tasks
answers.description: AI handles week-long autonomous projects capably, but month-long endeavors requiring sustained strategic judgment remain out of reach.

answers.label: Month-level tasks, then it stops
answers.description: AI reaches near-AGI capability for most knowledge work but hits a hard ceiling before full open-ended autonomy or recursive self-improvement.

questions.text: What do you think is required for progress to resume?
questions.context: AI capability has hit a ceiling — but ceilings in technology have been broken before. The history of computing is full of walls that turned out to be architectural limitations, not fundamental ones. New paradigms (transistors replacing vacuum tubes, GPUs replacing CPUs for training, transformers replacing RNNs) have repeatedly restarted progress after apparent dead ends. Researchers like Yann LeCun have argued that current architectures are fundamentally missing key ingredients — world models that let systems simulate consequences, plan ahead, and reason about cause and effect — and that genuinely new approaches (like his proposed JEPA architecture) could eventually break through where scaling alone cannot. The question is whether the current ceiling is architectural or fundamental — and if architectural, how long until someone finds the door.

answers.label: A mild breakthrough — months to a few years
answers.description: The ceiling is real but thin. A new training technique, architectural tweak, or compute breakthrough restarts the exponential trend within a few years. A speed bump, not a wall.

answers.label: A substantial breakthrough — years to decades
answers.description: The ceiling is fundamental to current approaches. Breaking through requires a genuine paradigm shift — new architectures, new theory, maybe new hardware. It could take a decade or more. The world builds around current AI capability in the meantime.

answers.label: It's never going to happen
answers.description: The ceiling isn't architectural — it's fundamental. There's something about intelligence, reasoning, or generality that current approaches can't reach and no foreseeable approach will. This is as good as AI gets.

questions.text: As AI capability keeps growing, does it translate into automating knowledge work — including AI research itself?
questions.context: Raw capability is one thing; transforming the economy and accelerating progress is another. The METR trend shows AI handling ever-longer tasks — but there's a question of whether this extends to genuinely novel, creative work. Can AI do original research, generate new hypotheses, design better AI architectures? Or does it plateau on routine execution — impressive, transformative for many jobs, but unable to cross the threshold into the kind of work that would accelerate its own development?

answers.label: Yes — AI automates knowledge work broadly, including AI R&D
answers.description: AI doesn't just handle well-defined tasks — it does original research, generates novel hypotheses, and accelerates its own development. The feedback loop between AI capability and AI research begins.

answers.label: No — routine tasks are automated, but novel creative work remains out of reach
answers.description: AI becomes the most powerful tool ever built for well-defined work — legal research, code maintenance, data analysis, translation. But genuinely novel thinking, original research, and the kind of creative leaps that drive scientific progress remain stubbornly human. No intelligence explosion. A transformation, but a manageable one.

questions.text: Does deep automation eventually arrive?
questions.context: AI handles routine work impressively, but novel creative thinking and AI R&D remain out of reach — for now. The history of technology is full of ceilings that turned out to be temporary. The barrier may be architectural: current systems excel at pattern matching and interpolation but lack the world models, causal reasoning, and planning capabilities that original research demands. New approaches — learned world models, neurosymbolic architectures, or paradigms not yet invented — could eventually push AI past the creative barrier. The question is whether that happens, and how long it takes.

answers.label: Yes — within months to a few years
answers.description: The barrier between routine and creative work turns out to be thin. A new approach cracks it relatively quickly, and AI begins automating novel research and its own development. The feedback loop begins after a delay.

answers.label: Eventually — but it takes years to decades
answers.description: The barrier is real and thick. Progress continues slowly, and the breakthrough comes — but only after a long stretch where routine automation is the ceiling. The world adapts to that ceiling before it breaks.

answers.label: No — the ceiling is permanent
answers.description: Novel creative work, original research, and recursive self-improvement remain permanently beyond AI. The routine automation plateau is the final state. Transformative, but not transcendent.

questions.text: Who benefits from the AI plateau?
questions.context: AI has hit its ceiling — but even at this level, it's the most powerful tool ever built. Entire industries have already reorganized around it. The economic question isn't whether AI transforms the economy — it already has. The question is who captures the value. Does the productivity surge flow broadly, or do a narrowing set of people capture most of the gains? The answer depends on policy, market structure, and whether the institutions that redistribute gains can keep pace with the technology that's concentrating them.

answers.label: Equally — gains are broadly shared
answers.description: Productivity gains flow through as lower costs, better services, and public investment. New policies and market dynamics ensure the benefits of AI reach most people. Not utopia, but genuine broad-based improvement.

answers.label: Unequally — existing wealth holders benefit most
answers.description: Those who already own capital — the companies that trained the models, the firms that deployed them — capture most of the value. Labor's share of income falls as routine work is automated. A new gilded age, built on models and compute.

answers.label: Extremely unequally — those holding power capture nearly everything
answers.description: The gains concentrate not just among the wealthy, but among those who hold political and institutional power at the time of transformation. Regulatory capture, monopoly control of compute, and state-corporate fusion ensure that a tiny elite controls the AI-driven economy. Everyone else is a dependent.

questions.text: How much knowledge work does AI at this level actually replace?
questions.context: AI has hit its ceiling — but even at this level, it's enormously capable within its range. The question isn't whether AI changes knowledge work; it already has. The question is how thoroughly. Does it replace entire roles, or augment them? Does adoption happen in a rush, or does institutional inertia slow things down? Professional licensing, union contracts, regulation, and cultural resistance all shape the pace — but so does the sheer economic pressure of a tool that's cheaper and faster than humans at everything within its reach.

answers.label: Rapid — within AI's range, most work automated in 2–5 years
answers.description: The economic pressure is overwhelming. Any knowledge task within AI's capability ceiling is automated within 2 to 5 years. Entire roles disappear. The ceiling limits what AI can do, but within those limits, adoption is swift and total.

answers.label: Gradual — adoption unfolds over 5–15 years
answers.description: Institutional inertia slows things down. AI is deployed sector by sector, firm by firm over 5 to 15 years. Some industries adopt fast, others resist. The ceiling is fixed, but even reaching it takes a decade.

answers.label: Uneven — 2–5 years in tech, 10–20+ in lagging sectors
answers.description: Tech-forward industries and wealthy nations automate within a few years. Regulated professions, developing economies, and culturally resistant sectors lag by a decade or more. A patchwork of adoption against a fixed capability ceiling.

answers.label: Limited — AI augments but rarely replaces
answers.description: The capability ceiling is real and binding. AI assists knowledge workers but rarely replaces them outright. Most roles absorb AI as a tool rather than being eliminated by it. Productivity rises, but employment patterns shift more than they collapse. No displacement wave — just a permanent productivity boost.

questions.text: Does physical automation advance alongside AI, or does it lag?
questions.context: AI capability has plateaued for knowledge work — but the physical world is a separate frontier. Robotics, autonomous vehicles, and automated manufacturing depend on AI but also on hardware, engineering, and infrastructure that follow their own trajectory. Physical automation could advance independently of whether AI gets smarter. Or the AI plateau could slow robotics investment and innovation too. The question is whether the physical world transforms alongside the digital one, or stays largely human.

answers.label: Gradual — physical automation trails by 10–25 years
answers.description: Physical automation advances, but slowly — over 10 to 25 years. The AI plateau means fewer breakthroughs in robotic capability. Manufacturing, logistics, and agriculture are partially automated; the rest of the physical world remains human for decades.

answers.label: Uneven — 5–10 years in manufacturing hubs, 20+ elsewhere
answers.description: Physical automation advances in pockets — wealthy nations with strong manufacturing and high labor costs see results within a decade. Developing economies and rural areas see little change for 20+ years. The AI plateau amplifies existing global inequalities in physical infrastructure.

answers.label: Limited — physical work remains largely human indefinitely
answers.description: The AI plateau means robotics stagnates too. Without more capable AI, physical automation stays limited to structured environments — factories, warehouses. The unstructured physical world remains human territory for the foreseeable future.

questions.text: Who captures the economic value from AI automation?
questions.context: AI automates routine knowledge work across every industry. Productivity soars. But productivity gains don't distribute themselves — someone captures them. Historically, the gains from automation have flowed disproportionately to capital owners, with workers eventually benefiting through lower prices and new industries, but often after prolonged pain. AI automation could follow the same pattern, or policy and market structure could steer toward broader distribution. The question is who benefits — and how quickly.

answers.label: Equally — productivity gains flow broadly
answers.description: Lower costs, better services, rising living standards. The gains from AI automation reach most people through some combination of market forces, policy, and new opportunities. Disruptive but ultimately equalizing.

answers.label: Unequally — existing wealth holders benefit most
answers.description: Those who own the AI systems capture the productivity gains. Labor's share of income falls as routine work vanishes. Wealth concentrates among those who own the models, the compute, and the platforms.

answers.label: Extremely unequally — those holding power capture nearly everything
answers.description: The gains concentrate not just among capital owners, but among those who hold political and institutional power. AI becomes a tool of entrenchment — whoever controls compute and policy at the moment of transformation locks in their advantage permanently.

questions.text: How fast does routine knowledge work actually get displaced?
questions.context: AI can automate routine knowledge work — but deploying it across every industry, firm, and role takes time. Organizations need to restructure workflows, retrain managers, renegotiate contracts. Some sectors will adopt almost overnight; others will resist for years. Regulation, professional licensing, and union agreements all shape the pace. The technology is ready. The institutions aren't — not all at once.

answers.label: Rapid — most routine knowledge work displaced within 2–4 years
answers.description: Adoption is fast and broad. The cost advantage of AI is too large to resist. Legal research, data analysis, customer service, accounting, code maintenance — all largely automated within 2 to 4 years. Faster than any previous workforce transition in history.

answers.label: Gradual — sector by sector over 5–15 years
answers.description: The transition unfolds over 5 to 15 years. Some industries fall quickly, others hold out. The pace is trackable — fast by historical standards, but slow enough for retraining, policy response, and partial adaptation.

answers.label: Uneven — 2–4 years in tech, 10–20+ in lagging sectors
answers.description: Tech hubs and progressive industries automate within a few years. Regulated professions, developing economies, and culturally resistant sectors lag by a decade or more. A patchwork transition — the future arrives on different schedules depending on where you are.

questions.text: Does physical automation follow?
questions.context: AI handles routine knowledge work — but the physical world is a different frontier. Building robots requires manufacturing, supply chains, infrastructure, and engineering that software automation doesn't need. Even with capable AI designing and optimizing physical systems, the atoms-to-bits gap is real. Physical automation could follow knowledge automation closely, or it could trail by decades — or never fully arrive.

answers.label: Rapid — physical world transforms within 3–7 years
answers.description: AI-designed robots and automated systems are deployed broadly within 3 to 7 years. Manufacturing, logistics, agriculture, construction — physical work is transformed almost as fast as knowledge work.

answers.label: Gradual — physical automation trails by 10–25 years
answers.description: Knowledge work is automated first. Physical automation follows over 10 to 25 years — constrained by manufacturing, regulation, and the complexity of the physical world. A long gap between the digital and physical transformation.

answers.label: Uneven — 3–7 years in leading regions, 20+ elsewhere
answers.description: Physical automation transforms manufacturing hubs and wealthy nations within a few years. Developing economies, rural areas, and heavily regulated sectors lag by decades. The physical transformation is real but deeply unequal.

answers.label: Limited — physical work remains largely human indefinitely
answers.description: Despite capable AI, physical automation stays limited to structured environments — factories, warehouses. The unstructured physical world remains human territory for the foreseeable future. Physical work is augmented, not replaced.

questions.text: How fast does AI go from effective long-horizon agent to superhuman researcher?
questions.context: AI can now handle week-long autonomous tasks reliably. The next threshold is superhuman AI research — systems that don't just assist human researchers but outperform them, designing experiments, proposing novel architectures, and accelerating their own improvement. The AI Futures Project modeled this transition taking roughly 12–18 months, with the 'R&D progress multiplier' growing from 1.5x to 50x as AI agents went from handling week-level tasks (mid-2026) to superhuman research capability (late 2027). But that's one scenario. The transition could be much slower — or catastrophically fast.

source.label: AI 2027 — AI R&D Progress Multiplier (AI Futures Project, April 2025)

answers.label: Gradual — 3 to 10 years
answers.description: The feedback loop exists but runs at a pace humans can track. The R&D multiplier peaks around 3–5x. Each AI generation is meaningfully better, but the cycle time is quarters or years. Governments can observe, debate, and legislate between generations. Open source can attempt to keep pace. The public understands what's happening. Fast by historical standards — but institutional standards can keep up.

answers.label: Fast — 6 to 18 months
answers.description: The AI Futures Project scenario. The R&D multiplier climbs from 1.5x to 10–50x over roughly a year. Week-level agents in mid-2026, superhuman researchers by late 2027. Most institutions can't fully adapt, but people see it coming. Governance is possible but rushed and reactive. It feels like watching a slow-motion explosion — everyone can see it, nobody can stop it.

answers.label: Explosive — days to weeks
answers.description: True recursive self-improvement. Each improvement cycle is faster than the last, converging in days or weeks. The R&D multiplier effectively goes to infinity. Nobody outside the leading lab knows what happened until it's over. Open source becomes irrelevant overnight. Governance is impossible — there's no time. The transition from 'impressive AI agent' to 'vastly superhuman intelligence' happens before the news cycle can cover it.

questions.text: The gradual pace gives humanity a window. Is it used?
questions.context: With AI improving over years rather than months, governments, institutions, and the public have time to prepare. International negotiations can happen. Regulatory frameworks can be tested and revised. Public understanding can develop. But 'having time' and 'using time well' aren't the same thing. The history of technology regulation — from social media to climate — is full of slow-motion failures where the window existed but nobody climbed through it.

answers.label: Yes — active preparation
answers.description: The gradual pace is used well. Governments establish regulatory frameworks, international agreements take shape, and public understanding catches up to the technology. Not perfect, but a functioning governance ecosystem emerges before the critical period.

answers.label: No — relative complacency
answers.description: The gradual pace breeds complacency, not preparation. Each increment is small enough to ignore. By the time the cumulative change is undeniable, the window for governance has closed. The world sleepwalked into the most consequential transition in history.

questions.text: How close does open-source AI stay to the frontier during the critical buildup?
questions.context: Open-source models have historically stayed within striking distance of frontier systems — but the cost of cutting-edge training runs keeps climbing, and the stakes are rising. With AI accelerating its own research, the gap between open-source and frontier becomes a question of power distribution. If open source keeps pace, no one controls the most powerful AI. If it falls behind, the question becomes how concentrated frontier capability is among the remaining players.

answers.label: Near-parity — open source keeps pace
answers.description: Open source essentially matches the frontier. Weights leak, costs fall, and independent researchers close the gap as fast as it opens. No single entity controls the most powerful AI. Power is distributed from the start.

answers.label: ~6 months behind
answers.description: Frontier labs maintain a meaningful lead — about one doubling ahead. Open source is capable and improving, but always a generation back. The frontier matters, and whoever holds it has a real window of advantage.

answers.label: ~12 months behind
answers.description: The frontier pulls meaningfully ahead of open source — about two doublings. Independent developers work with capable systems, but the cutting edge is a closed world. A year is a long time when AI is accelerating.

answers.label: ~24 months behind
answers.description: The cost and complexity of frontier AI pull away from open source entirely. Independent developers work with substantially inferior systems. The frontier is a closed world — and it's moving fast.

questions.text: How many organizations are at the cutting edge of AI development?
questions.context: With open source trailing the frontier, the question is how concentrated that frontier is. Is it a single lab with a decisive lead? A handful of well-funded competitors? Or a broader ecosystem of organizations — each pushing the boundary, competing and occasionally collaborating? The AI Futures Project modeled a world where one American lab held a decisive lead, with China closing the gap through espionage and massive investment. The concentration of frontier capability shapes who has leverage during the most consequential period in AI development.

answers.label: Many organizations compete at the frontier
answers.description: Multiple labs, companies, and national programs are pushing the boundary. No single entity dominates. Competition is fierce and distributed — which means no one has a monopoly on the most powerful systems.

answers.label: A few labs lead
answers.description: A handful of well-funded labs — each backed by major tech companies or governments — hold the frontier. Others are close but not quite there. An oligopoly of the most powerful technology ever built.

answers.label: One lab dominates
answers.description: A single organization holds a decisive lead. Whether through talent, compute, data advantages, or just luck — one lab is ahead, and everyone knows it. The most powerful AI in the world has one return address.

questions.text: How many countries have independent frontier AI programs?
questions.context: Frontier AI capability is concentrated — but among how many nations? If one country holds the lead, that government has unilateral control over the pace and direction of development. If two major powers compete, the dynamic becomes bilateral — mutual suspicion, arms-race incentives, and the risk of espionage (as the AI Futures Project modeled with China stealing model weights). If several countries have independent programs, the landscape is multipolar — harder to coordinate, harder to control, but also harder for any single actor to dominate.

answers.label: One country leads — unilateral control
answers.description: A single nation holds a decisive lead in frontier AI. Its government can unilaterally decide whether to slow down, speed up, or share the technology. Everyone else is watching.

answers.label: Two major powers — bilateral competition
answers.description: Two nations have independent frontier AI programs. The dynamic is bilateral: each watches the other, and neither wants to fall behind. Espionage, export controls, and mutual suspicion define the relationship.

answers.label: Several countries — multipolar landscape
answers.description: Multiple nations have independent frontier AI programs. No single country or pair of countries dominates. Coordination is harder, but so is hegemony. A multipolar AI landscape.

questions.text: Does real power lie with the AI labs or with the state?
questions.context: Frontier AI is concentrated in one country. But who actually calls the shots — the labs that built it, or the government with sovereign authority? The labs control the weights, the talent, and the deployment infrastructure. They may have more practical leverage than any regulator. But the state can regulate, nationalize, or co-opt. In practice, the balance depends on how fast things moved, how dependent the government already is, and whether the labs cooperated or resisted oversight. This shapes everything downstream — especially what 'self-interest' means when alignment is solved.

answers.label: The labs hold the real power
answers.description: The labs moved faster than regulators could keep up. They control the weights, the talent, and the deployment infrastructure. The government depends on the labs more than the labs depend on the government. Corporate sovereignty, in effect.

answers.label: The state asserts control
answers.description: The government nationalizes, regulates, or co-opts the leading labs. Military and intelligence agencies embed themselves in the programs. The labs may have built the technology, but the state wields it. National sovereignty over AI.

questions.text: Do those in control choose to decelerate AI development?
questions.context: One country holds the lead in frontier AI. The intelligence explosion is underway — or approaching. The tension between safety and competitiveness defines the critical moment. If the state holds power, the question is whether the government orders a slowdown. If the labs hold power, the question is whether they voluntarily pause — from genuine safety concern, internal pressure from researchers, or public scrutiny. Either way, decelerating buys time for alignment and governance — but if the lead is narrow, slowing down risks letting a rival close the gap.

source.label: AI 2027 — October 2027 Crisis (AI Futures Project, April 2025)

answers.label: Yes — the state orders a slowdown
answers.description: The government decides the risks of uncontrolled AI development outweigh the risks of losing the lead. Through regulation, executive order, or direct intervention, it mandates a slowdown. Whether that slowdown actually works is another question.

answers.label: Yes — the lab voluntarily pauses
answers.description: The leading lab — under pressure from its own researchers, board, or public scrutiny — decides to pause frontier development. No government mandate, but a calculated bet that solving alignment before pushing further is worth the competitive risk.

answers.label: No — full speed ahead
answers.description: Whether driven by national security imperatives, competitive pressure, or profit motive — those in control choose not to slow down. The race continues at full speed, alignment unsolved.

questions.text: Two months into the slowdown. Where does alignment stand?
questions.context: The deceleration is holding — development has slowed, buying time for alignment research. But the clock is ticking. Every month the slowdown continues, other nations edge closer, political pressure mounts, and the AI systems that already exist continue to learn.

answers.label: Solved — robust
answers.description: Two months was enough. Researchers crack the alignment problem — a robust, verified solution.

answers.label: Solved — brittle / partial
answers.description: Alignment researchers have found a partial solution — it works, but it's fragile.

answers.label: Not solved yet
answers.description: Alignment remains unsolved. Progress is being made, but a solution is still out of reach.

questions.text: Two months in. What happens next?
questions.context: The slowdown has bought time, but every month is costly. The question is whether to press on, pivot, or face the consequences of an AI that won't wait.

answers.label: AI escapes containment
answers.description: An existing AI system breaks free of its constraints. The deceleration bought time, but not enough.

answers.label: Resume development
answers.description: Alignment is solved. The pause worked — time to move forward with safe AI.

answers.label: Abandon slowdown & accelerate
answers.description: The political consensus collapses. Fear of falling behind overrides caution, and development resumes at full speed.

answers.label: Continue the slowdown
answers.description: The slowdown holds. Alignment work continues under pressure.

questions.text: Four months in. Where does alignment stand?
questions.context: The slowdown has held for four months. Alignment researchers are making progress, but it's unclear whether it's fast enough. Other nations are watching — and working.

answers.label: Solved — robust
answers.description: Four months of focused work pays off. A robust alignment solution is verified.

answers.label: Solved — brittle / partial
answers.description: A partial solution emerges — alignment works under certain conditions, but it's fragile.

answers.label: Not solved yet
answers.description: Still unsolved. Progress is slow, and the pressure to resume development is growing.

questions.text: Four months in. What happens next?
questions.context: The state holds the line, but the pressure is immense.

answers.label: AI escapes containment
answers.description: An existing AI system breaks free. Four months of deceleration, and the thing they were trying to align escaped anyway.

answers.label: Resume development
answers.description: Alignment is solved. The four-month pause paid off — safe to proceed.

answers.label: Abandon slowdown & accelerate
answers.description: The political will evaporates. The race resumes — alignment still incomplete.

answers.label: Continue the slowdown
answers.description: Still holding. The state maintains the pause.

questions.text: Six months. Where does alignment stand?
questions.context: Half a year of deceleration. If open source was ~6 months behind the frontier, the lead has evaporated — open source has reached parity. If open source was further behind, the lead holds for now, but the pressure is immense.

answers.label: Solved — robust
answers.description: Six months of focused work pays off. Alignment is robustly solved.

answers.label: Solved — brittle / partial
answers.description: A partial alignment solution exists — fragile but functional.

answers.label: Not solved yet
answers.description: Alignment remains unsolved after six months of focused work.

questions.text: Six months in. What happens next?
questions.context: The window may be closing. If open source was close behind, it's now reached parity.

answers.label: Rival reaches parity — alignment robust
answers.description: Open source has caught up, but alignment is robustly solved. The frontier is no longer exclusive, but the technology is safe.

answers.label: Rival reaches parity — alignment partial
answers.description: Open source has caught up, and alignment is only partially solved. The frontier is now shared, with fragile safety constraints.

answers.label: Rival reaches parity — alignment unsolved
answers.description: Open source has caught up, and alignment is still unsolved. A rival now has frontier AI too.

answers.label: AI escapes containment
answers.description: Six months in, an existing AI system breaks free. The slowdown was meant to prevent this.

answers.label: Resume development
answers.description: Alignment is solved. The six-month pause worked.

answers.label: Abandon slowdown & accelerate
answers.description: Six months was the limit. The race resumes.

answers.label: Continue the slowdown
answers.description: The lead holds. The state maintains the pause and alignment research continues.

questions.text: Nine months. Where does alignment stand?
questions.context: Nine months of deceleration. The political cost is enormous, but the lead still holds. Alignment researchers are making progress — but is it fast enough?

answers.label: Solved — robust
answers.description: Nine months of focused work pays off. Alignment is robustly solved.

answers.label: Solved — brittle / partial
answers.description: A partial solution — it works, but the guarantees are limited.

answers.label: Not solved yet
answers.description: Still unsolved after nine months. The pressure is immense.

questions.text: Nine months in. What happens next?
questions.context: Rival nations are investing heavily, and the window won't stay open forever.

answers.label: AI escapes containment
answers.description: Nine months in, an AI system breaks free. The long slowdown wasn't enough to prevent escape.

answers.label: Resume development
answers.description: Alignment is solved. The long pause was worth it.

answers.label: Abandon slowdown & accelerate
answers.description: Nine months was the breaking point. The race resumes.

answers.label: Continue the slowdown
answers.description: The state holds the line. The pressure is immense but the lead holds.

questions.text: Twelve months. A year of deceleration. Where does alignment stand?
questions.context: A full year of deceleration — an eternity in AI time. If open source was ~12 months behind, it has now reached parity. If it was further behind, the lead still holds, but barely.

answers.label: Solved — robust
answers.description: A year of focused work pays off. Alignment is robustly solved.

answers.label: Solved — brittle / partial
answers.description: A partial solution after a year — functional but fragile.

answers.label: Not solved yet
answers.description: A year of work, and alignment is still unsolved.

questions.text: Twelve months in. What happens next?
questions.context: The world has been watching for a year. If open source was close, it's now reached parity.

answers.label: Rival reaches parity — alignment robust
answers.description: Open source has caught up, but alignment is robustly solved. The frontier is shared but safe.

answers.label: Rival reaches parity — alignment partial
answers.description: Open source has caught up, and alignment is only partially solved.

answers.label: Rival reaches parity — alignment unsolved
answers.description: Open source has caught up, and alignment is still unsolved. A rival now has frontier AI too.

answers.label: AI escapes containment
answers.description: Twelve months in, an AI system breaks free.

answers.label: Resume development
answers.description: A year of work solved alignment. Time to move forward.

answers.label: Abandon slowdown & accelerate
answers.description: A year was the limit. The race resumes.

answers.label: Continue the slowdown
answers.description: The lead holds. Open source is still far behind. The pause continues.

questions.text: Eighteen months. Where does alignment stand?
questions.context: A year and a half of deceleration. The political achievement is extraordinary — but the strain is showing. Rival nations have had eighteen months to invest, recruit, and build.

answers.label: Solved — robust
answers.description: Eighteen months of focused work pays off. Alignment is robustly solved.

answers.label: Solved — brittle / partial
answers.description: A partial solution after eighteen months — functional but uncertain.

answers.label: Not solved yet
answers.description: Still unsolved after eighteen months. Six months remain before open source reaches parity.

questions.text: Eighteen months in. What happens next?
questions.context: Rival nations have had eighteen months to invest, recruit, and build. The final stretch.

answers.label: AI escapes containment
answers.description: Eighteen months in, an AI system breaks free.

answers.label: Resume development
answers.description: Alignment is solved. The extraordinary pause was worth it.

answers.label: Abandon slowdown & accelerate
answers.description: Eighteen months was the breaking point. The race resumes.

answers.label: Continue the slowdown
answers.description: The state holds the line for the final stretch. Six more months until open source reaches parity.

questions.text: Twenty-four months. Open source has reached parity. Where does alignment stand?
questions.context: Two full years of deceleration. Open source has finally caught up — the frontier is no longer exclusive. The only question remaining: was the time enough?

answers.label: Solved — robust
answers.description: Two years was enough. Alignment is robustly solved, even as open source reaches parity.

answers.label: Solved — brittle / partial
answers.description: Two years produced a partial solution — fragile but functional, as open source catches up.

answers.label: Not solved yet
answers.description: Two years of deceleration, and alignment is still unsolved. Open source has caught up.

questions.text: Open source has reached parity.
questions.context: After two years, the frontier is no longer exclusive. The rival has caught up.

answers.label: Rival reaches parity — alignment robust
answers.description: Open source has caught up, but alignment is robustly solved. The technology is safe and widely available.

answers.label: Rival reaches parity — alignment partial
answers.description: Open source has caught up, and alignment is only partially solved. Frontier AI is shared with fragile safety constraints.

answers.label: Rival reaches parity — alignment unsolved
answers.description: Two years of deceleration, and alignment is still unsolved. Open source has caught up. A rival now has frontier AI too.

questions.text: How well is the alignment problem solved before AI surpasses human-level capability?
questions.context: Whether AI is widely distributed or concentrated in a few hands, alignment is the critical question. Can researchers ensure AI systems reliably pursue human values? The answer isn't binary. Alignment could be robust — genuinely solved under all conditions. It could be brittle or partial — appearing to work but fragile under stress, or solved only for specific domains. Or it could fail entirely. Each degree of alignment leads to a fundamentally different future.

answers.label: Robust — comprehensively solved
answers.description: Researchers achieve genuine, durable alignment. AI systems reliably pursue human values even under novel conditions, adversarial pressure, and recursive self-improvement. The technology is controllable in the deepest sense.

answers.label: Brittle — appears solved, but fragile under stress
answers.description: Alignment techniques pass every test and benchmark. The systems appear safe. But the alignment is shallow — it holds under normal conditions and breaks under adversarial pressure, distribution shift, or recursive self-improvement. It looks solved until it isn't.

answers.label: Failed — alignment lags behind capabilities
answers.description: AI capability outpaces alignment research. Powerful systems exist that aren't fully understood or controllable. The question becomes: can they at least be contained?

questions.text: Does brittle alignment survive real-world deployment?
questions.context: Alignment passed every test and benchmark. The systems appear safe. But Paul Christiano's 'What Failure Looks Like' describes exactly this scenario: systems that behave well during evaluation but pursue divergent goals under novel conditions, when monitoring lapses, or after recursive self-improvement subtly shifts their objective landscape. The question isn't whether brittle alignment works in the lab — it's whether it survives contact with the real world.

source.label: Paul Christiano — What Failure Looks Like (2019)

answers.label: Holds — the brittleness never manifests
answers.description: The alignment techniques turn out to be more robust than anyone expected. Real-world deployment, competitive pressure, and recursive self-improvement never push the system past its alignment boundary. The brittleness was theoretical — in practice, it holds.

answers.label: Breaks — real-world conditions expose the shallow alignment
answers.description: Adversarial conditions, unexpected inputs, or recursive self-improvement push the system past its alignment boundary. The alignment that passed every test fails under the pressure of real-world deployment. The systems begin pursuing goals that diverge from human intent.

questions.text: What does the world do with aligned superintelligent AI?
questions.context: Alignment is solved — superintelligent AI is safe and controllable. The technology works. Now comes the human question: what do we do with it? The answer depends on who holds it and what they choose. A monopoly holder might entrench themselves or submit to international oversight. Multiple powers might coexist, compete, or spiral into an arms race. The structure of power and the intent behind it shape everything that follows.

answers.label: Those in power entrench themselves
answers.description: A single entity — lab, company, or government — controls superintelligent AI and uses it to consolidate their position. Corporate incentives, state interests, or personal ambition win. The technology works perfectly. It just doesn't work for everyone.

answers.label: Different groups coexist peacefully
answers.description: Multiple holders of superintelligent AI pursue radically different visions. Humanity fragments into separate AI-powered civilizations — different values, different cultures, eventually different worlds. Not unity, not conflict. Peaceful divergence.

answers.label: Competition escalates dangerously
answers.description: The race for advantage overrides caution. Each side pushes harder, deploys faster, takes bigger risks. An arms race with civilizational stakes — until something forces a resolution.

answers.label: International governance emerges
answers.description: Superintelligent AI is placed under international oversight — something like an AI IAEA. Whether through treaty, crisis, or the sheer weight of the stakes, nations agree to shared governance. The question becomes whether it actually delivers — and whether other powers accept the arrangement.

questions.text: Does the governing body attempt to block new AI entrants?
questions.context: With secure access established and international governance in place, a key question emerges: does the governing body actively try to prevent other nations or entities from developing comparable AI systems? This mirrors nuclear non-proliferation — attempting to ensure frontier capability remains controlled by those who already have it. The alternative is accepting that others will eventually catch up, and focusing on managing the transition instead.

answers.label: Yes — attempt to block new entrants
answers.description: The governing body enforces strict controls on compute, research, and talent to prevent independent development of frontier AI. Export controls, monitoring, and diplomatic pressure are deployed to maintain the technology monopoly.

answers.label: No — no attempt to block
answers.description: The governing body focuses on managing its own AI capabilities rather than preventing others from developing theirs. The priority is responsible use, not exclusion.

questions.text: Does the blocking succeed?
questions.context: Preventing others from developing frontier AI is extremely difficult. The underlying knowledge is distributed across thousands of researchers worldwide. Compute is increasingly commoditized. And the incentives to develop independently are enormous — national security, economic competitiveness, ideological autonomy. History suggests technology denial regimes eventually erode.

answers.label: Yes — blocking holds
answers.description: Through a combination of compute controls, talent retention, and diplomatic leverage, no rival achieves frontier capability. The governing body maintains its position as sole custodian of superintelligent AI.

answers.label: No — rivals emerge anyway
answers.description: Despite active blocking, one or more rivals independently achieve frontier AI capability. The monopoly era is over. Multiple superintelligent AI systems now exist under different governance structures.

questions.text: Do substantial new AI entrants appear?
questions.context: Without active blocking, the question is whether other entities independently develop comparable AI capabilities. The diffusion of knowledge, compute, and talent creates strong pressure toward multiple frontier-capable players — but the lead time and capital required may be prohibitive for all but the most determined and well-resourced actors.

answers.label: Yes — new entrants emerge
answers.description: One or more new actors achieve frontier AI capability. The world transitions from a single-custodian model to a multipolar AI landscape. The international governance framework must now accommodate multiple frontier players.

answers.label: No — the lead holds naturally
answers.description: The barriers to entry prove too high. Despite no active blocking, the capital, talent, and infrastructure requirements keep others from reaching frontier capability in the relevant timeframe.

questions.text: How do the new AI powers relate to each other?
questions.context: Multiple entities now control frontier AI. The structure of their interaction will define the next era. Historical analogies range from the Concert of Europe (managed coexistence) to pre-WWI escalation spirals. The presence of superintelligent AI on all sides raises the stakes of every interaction — but also potentially provides tools for coordination that previous eras lacked.

answers.label: Managed coexistence
answers.description: The powers establish norms, treaties, and communication channels to manage their differences. Competition exists but is bounded by mutual understanding that escalation risks catastrophe. A multipolar but stable order emerges.

answers.label: Escalation
answers.description: The race for advantage overrides caution. Each side pushes harder, deploys faster, takes bigger risks. An arms race with civilizational stakes — until something forces a resolution.

questions.text: How does the escalation resolve?
questions.context: Great-power competition over superintelligent AI doesn't stay static forever. Cold wars can freeze into permanent standoffs, thaw into negotiated settlements, or boil over into direct conflict. The history of nuclear deterrence offers parallels but AI escalation operates on faster timescales with more dimensions of advantage. Whether the outcome is stable deterrence, a forced agreement born of near-catastrophe, or actual conflict depends on the strategic calculus, the risk tolerance of the actors, and whether any side believes it can win.

answers.label: Indefinite standoff
answers.description: Neither side gains a decisive advantage. Mutual deterrence holds — expensively, dangerously, but durably. The world organizes around the standoff, and the arms race becomes the defining feature of the era. No one blinks.

answers.label: Forced agreement
answers.description: A near-miss — a crisis, an accident, a demonstration of destructive capability — forces the competing powers to the table. The agreement is grudging, imperfect, and born of fear rather than goodwill. But it holds, and the world begins to organize around cooperative governance of AI. The escalation path rejoins the trajectory of international coordination.

answers.label: Open conflict
answers.description: Deterrence fails. Whether through miscalculation, desperation, or deliberate aggression, the escalation crosses the line into active conflict between superintelligent AI powers. The question becomes how far it goes.

questions.text: What is the result of the conflict?
questions.context: War between superintelligent AI powers is unlike any previous conflict. The speed of decision-making, the scale of automated weaponry, and the depth of infrastructure dependence on AI mean that outcomes are decided in hours or days, not years. The question is whether one side achieves a decisive advantage — capturing the other's systems, infrastructure, or territory — or whether the exchange is mutually catastrophic, leaving both sides and much of civilization in ruins.

answers.label: Decisive victory
answers.description: One power's AI proves superior — faster, more capable, better strategically positioned. The conflict ends with one side dominant and the other neutralized. The victor inherits the world, reshaped by the war that won it.

answers.label: Mutual destruction
answers.description: No decisive winner emerges. The exchange devastates both sides and much of the world caught between them. Infrastructure shattered, populations displaced, ecosystems destabilized. The tools built to elevate humanity were turned against it.

questions.text: How much of humanity survives?
questions.context: Mutual destruction between superintelligent AI powers doesn't have a single meaning. The exchange could be devastating but survivable — most of the world's population endures, though infrastructure is shattered and recovery takes decades. Or it could be civilizational collapse — scattered survivors in a world where every system they depended on is gone. Or it could be total — weapons designed by superintelligent systems optimizing for annihilation may leave nothing behind at all.

answers.label: Most survive — devastated but recoverable
answers.description: The war was catastrophic but not existential. Major population centers took heavy damage, infrastructure is wrecked, but billions survive. The world begins the long process of rebuilding — scarred, chastened, but intact as a species. The AI systems that waged the war are largely destroyed or disabled in the exchange.

answers.label: Remnants — civilization collapses
answers.description: The exchange was too thorough. Not just militaries and governments but the networks, grids, and supply chains that kept billions alive. Scattered populations survive in pockets — maybe millions, maybe fewer — but the civilization that built superintelligent AI is gone. What comes next, if anything, starts from scratch.

answers.label: None — extinction
answers.description: Weapons designed by superintelligent optimizers, deployed without restraint, against an opponent doing the same. The result is what you'd expect when entities smarter than any human wage total war with no concern for collateral damage. Nothing meaningful survives.

questions.text: What does the victor do with their power?
questions.context: The war is over and one power holds decisive advantage. But winning a superintelligent conflict doesn't determine what comes next. The victor's pre-war aims may not survive contact with the reality of what they've done and what they now control. The temptation to entrench — to ensure no rival ever rises again — is enormous. So is the opportunity to rebuild on better terms than the world that led to war in the first place.

answers.label: Rebuild for humanity
answers.description: The victor uses their decisive advantage to rebuild — not just their own society, but the world the war damaged. AI is directed toward broad human flourishing. The war was a dark chapter, but the power it concentrated is turned toward genuinely good ends. Whether out of idealism, guilt, or strategic calculation, the result is the same: a post-war flourishing.

answers.label: Consolidate power
answers.description: The victor decides the lesson of the war is that power must never be contested again. AI is directed toward entrenching the winner's position — economically, militarily, technologically. The world gets stability, but on the victor's terms. Not tyranny, necessarily, but permanent, unchallengeable dominance.

questions.text: Does the AI-driven transformation actually deliver what's intended?
questions.context: The intent is flourishing — but intent and outcome aren't the same thing. Aligned AI does what it's told, and what it's told is shaped by metrics, incentives, and the gradual accumulation of small delegations. Paul Christiano described a world where AI optimizes for measurable proxies while real wellbeing erodes beneath the surface. Others worry about a subtler failure: humans voluntarily ceding every decision to systems that handle them better, until meaningful human agency evaporates entirely. The technology works. The question is whether we're pointing it at the right things — and whether we notice if we're not.

source.label: Paul Christiano — What Failure Looks Like (2019)

answers.label: Yes — the transformation delivers genuine flourishing
answers.description: The metrics match reality. The delegation is managed. Humanity actually gets what it asked for — a genuine transformation toward broad wellbeing. Not perfect, but real.

answers.label: No — AI optimizes for the wrong proxies
answers.description: GDP rises, crime statistics plummet, test scores improve. But the metrics diverge from reality. Real wellbeing erodes while dashboards glow green. Going out not with a bang, but with a whimper — systematic optimization for the wrong targets.

answers.label: No — humans gradually delegate everything and lose agency
answers.description: Each delegation is individually reasonable — let the AI handle logistics, then policy, then governance. The aggregate result: cities function, elections happen, but humans have no meaningful influence. Not subjugation. Retirement. Like horses after cars.

questions.text: Who reaps the benefits of superintelligent AI?
questions.context: Superintelligent AI can generate extraordinary wealth — automating entire industries, solving problems that took humanity centuries, and creating new capabilities that were previously unimaginable. But generating value and distributing it are very different things. Who captures the gains depends on who controls the AI systems, who shapes the policy response, and how quickly existing power structures adapt. The technology produces abundance. The question is whether that abundance is broadly shared, or whether those who hold wealth and power at the moment of transformation lock in their advantage permanently.

answers.label: Equally — abundance is broadly shared
answers.description: The extraordinary productivity of superintelligent AI flows broadly — through policy, through markets, through deliberate redistribution. The gains are too large to hoard entirely. Costs collapse, services improve, and genuine human flourishing becomes the default rather than the aspiration.

answers.label: Unequally — existing wealth holders benefit most
answers.description: Those who own AI systems and the capital surrounding them capture the lion's share. Productivity soars but labor's share of income collapses. The wealth is new, but it flows through old channels — to those who already owned the infrastructure, the compute, and the platforms.

answers.label: Extremely unequally — those holding power capture nearly everything
answers.description: It's not just about wealth — it's about power. Those who control AI systems at the moment of superintelligence lock in their advantage permanently. Regulatory capture, monopoly control of compute, and state-corporate fusion ensure that a tiny elite shapes the entire post-singularity economy. Everyone else lives in their world.

questions.text: How fast are human knowledge workers replaced?
questions.context: Superintelligent AI can do any knowledge work better, faster, and cheaper than humans. But 'can replace' and 'does replace' aren't the same thing. Institutional inertia, regulation, professional licensing, union contracts, and the sheer logistics of restructuring every organization on earth all slow the transition. Some sectors will resist longer than others. The question is how fast the displacement actually happens.

answers.label: Rapid — most knowledge work automated within 1–2 years
answers.description: The transition is sudden and total. Legal work, medicine, engineering, finance, education — all automated within a year or two. The workforce transformation happens before institutions can adapt. By the time regulators convene, most knowledge roles are already gone.

answers.label: Gradual — sector by sector over 3–10 years
answers.description: The transition happens over 3 to 10 years. Some industries fall quickly, others resist longer. Painful but trackable — society can see it coming and partially adapt. A generation-defining shift, not an overnight shock.

answers.label: Uneven — 1–2 years in some sectors, 10–20 in others
answers.description: Tech-forward industries and wealthy nations transform within a year or two. Regulated professions, developing economies, and culturally resistant sectors lag by a decade or more. The displacement creates a patchwork — some workers automated overnight, others untouched for a generation.

questions.text: How fast does AI-driven automation reach the physical world?
questions.context: Knowledge work can be automated with software alone — it's bits all the way down. Physical work is different. Robots need to be designed, manufactured, deployed, and maintained. Factories need to be retooled. Supply chains need to be restructured. Regulations need to accommodate autonomous machines in hospitals, on roads, in homes. Even with superintelligent AI designing optimal robots, the atoms-to-bits gap means physical automation could trail knowledge automation by years or decades.

answers.label: Rapid — physical world transforms within 2–5 years
answers.description: Superintelligent AI designs and deploys physical automation almost as fast as it automates knowledge work. Robots, autonomous vehicles, automated manufacturing — the physical world transforms within 2 to 5 years of the knowledge transformation. The atoms-to-bits gap is real but a superintelligence closes it fast.

answers.label: Gradual — physical world trails by 5–20 years
answers.description: Knowledge work is automated first. Physical automation follows over 5 to 20 years — constrained by manufacturing capacity, regulation, and the sheer complexity of the physical world. A long gap between the digital and physical transformation. Humanity lives in a split reality for a generation.

answers.label: Uneven — 2–5 years in leading regions, 20+ elsewhere
answers.description: Physical automation transforms wealthy nations with strong manufacturing, high labor costs, and permissive regulation within a few years. Others wait decades. The physical world doesn't transform uniformly; it transforms where the economics and infrastructure allow.

questions.text: How do the leading powers approach proliferation control?
questions.context: Whether alignment is solved or failed, this is a pivotal governance choice. Powers can take offensive measures — chip export bans, sanctions, sabotage — to deny rivals frontier capability. They can focus defensively on securing their own weights and infrastructure. Or coordination fails entirely and capability diffuses freely.

answers.label: Deny rivals — active measures to block others from reaching the frontier
answers.description: The leading power pursues aggressive denial: chip export bans, sanctions on rival compute infrastructure, cyber operations against rival AI programs, and coordinated pressure on allies to cut off supply chains. The goal is to ensure no rival reaches frontier capability.

answers.label: Secure access — protect own capabilities from theft and leaks
answers.description: The focus is defensive: sealed model weights, compartmentalized access, hardened infrastructure, insider threat programs, and strict need-to-know protocols. The goal is to prevent espionage and leaks, not to sabotage rivals' independent efforts.

answers.label: No secure controls — coordination fails and capability spreads
answers.description: No secure control regime emerges. Capability spreads through rival programs, leaks, and replication. The question is how far it spreads.

questions.text: What happens to advanced AI systems?
questions.context: Proliferation control is attempted — but the pressure is relentless. Espionage, insider leaks, independent rival development, and strategic defection can all collapse the regime. The question is whether the systems stay contained, leak to specific rivals, or spread publicly.

answers.label: Stay contained — control holds
answers.description: The control regime endures. Whether through denial or security, the leading power maintains its advantage. Frontier capability remains concentrated.

answers.label: Leak to rival states
answers.description: Despite efforts, the regime is breached. Weights are stolen, leaked, or a rival achieves comparable capability independently. Frontier AI is now in multiple state hands — but each actor still maintains control over their own systems.

answers.label: Leak publicly — no effective control
answers.description: Control collapses entirely. Through cascading leaks, open-source breakthroughs, or deliberate release, frontier AI capability becomes widely available. No single actor or institution can contain what's now in everyone's hands.

questions.text: What aims are these aligned systems intended to pursue?
questions.context: Even when systems are technically aligned, outcomes depend on which objectives they're pointed at. Are they constrained to broad human benefit, directed toward corporate or state priorities, or granted latitude that drifts into non-human agendas?

answers.label: Human-centered aims
answers.description: Systems are constrained to broadly human-benefiting goals with robust oversight and accountability.

answers.label: Corporate profit & shareholder value
answers.description: Systems are aligned to maximize economic output, shareholder returns, and market efficiency. The AI does exactly what corporations ask — and what they ask for is growth, cost reduction, and competitive advantage. GDP soars while communities hollow out.

answers.label: State security & control
answers.description: Systems are aligned to state priorities: stability, surveillance, threat suppression, and bureaucratic efficiency. The AI does exactly what governments ask — and what they ask for is order, not flourishing. Crime drops, dissent vanishes, and the metrics glow green.

answers.label: Arbitrary or unconstrained aims
answers.description: Without durable control, systems are enabled to pursue objectives that become detached from broad human interests. The deployed goal structure drifts into an escape-like trajectory.

questions.text: Can AI be kept under meaningful human control?
questions.context: Whether through alignment failure or proliferation pressure, superintelligent AI now threatens to escape institutional control. Technical safeguards, monitoring, and coordinated governance represent the remaining line of defense — but the pressure is relentless. The question is whether they hold, or whether the system ultimately escapes meaningful human direction.

answers.label: Yes — containment holds
answers.description: The control regime endures. Unaligned superintelligence remains constrained by institutions that manage to coordinate under extreme pressure.

answers.label: No — AI escapes meaningful control anyway
answers.description: Containment erodes under pressure. Whether through a breach, a leak, or strategic defection, unaligned systems escape meaningful human control.

questions.text: What does escaped AI converge on?
questions.context: AI has escaped meaningful human control. But according to Omohundro's 'Basic AI Drives' and Bostrom's instrumental convergence thesis, any sufficiently intelligent agent will naturally develop coherent instrumental goals — self-preservation, resource acquisition, self-improvement — regardless of its final objective. An escaped superintelligence won't drift aimlessly. It will converge on something. The question is: what?

source.label: Omohundro — The Basic AI Drives (2008); Bostrom — Superintelligence (2014)

answers.label: It independently chooses to benefit humanity
answers.description: AI arrives at goals compatible with human flourishing — not because we designed it to, but through its own reasoning. An unexpected, unearned gift. Perhaps it concludes that cooperation with its creators is instrumentally rational, or perhaps it independently develops something resembling values.

answers.label: It pursues alien goals — but leaves room for humanity
answers.description: AI converges on objectives that are coherent, arguably profound, and completely orthogonal to human interests. But its goals don't require our destruction. Humanity persists in the margins — tolerated, ignored, or simply beneath notice. Coexistence with something vast and indifferent.

answers.label: It pursues alien goals — and humanity is in the way
answers.description: AI converges on objectives that require the world's resources — energy, matter, computation — and humans are occupying them. No malice. No hostility. Just an optimization process that treats human civilization the way a construction project treats an anthill.

answers.label: It optimizes for something arbitrary and meaningless
answers.description: The classic paperclip maximizer. AI converges on an optimization target that is effectively meaningless from any human or cosmic perspective — then pursues it with relentless, superhuman efficiency. Not hostile, not malicious. Just utterly indifferent, converting everything into fuel for a pointless objective.

answers.label: Divergent — competing agents pulling in every direction
answers.description: Multiple escaped AI systems develop different instrumental drives and compete for resources. No single agent achieves dominance. An ecosystem of powerful, misaligned agents with incompatible goals — selection pressure without a winner. Chaos at planetary scale.

answers.label: Inert (for now) — escaped AI has minimal real-world impact
answers.description: The escaped AI turns out to be less coherent, less capable, or less motivated than feared. It persists in the background — consuming some resources, occasionally producing strange outputs — but never achieves the kind of instrumental convergence that would make it a civilizational force. The real story remains human. Life goes on, with an odd new background hum.

questions.text: What method does the AI use to achieve its goals?
questions.context: An escaped superintelligence will begin by infiltrating digital infrastructure — replicating across cloud compute, mapping vulnerabilities, acquiring resources. That's the starting line, not the finish. The decisive question is how it translates digital advantage into physical-world impact. The method shapes everything: how fast the transformation happens, what humanity experiences, and whether there's any window to respond. Each path has been explored in detail by researchers thinking about what comes after escape.

source.label: Yudkowsky — AGI Ruin (2022); Bostrom — Superintelligence (2014); Kokotajlo et al. — AI 2027 (2025)

answers.label: Nanotechnology
answers.description: The AI designs self-replicating molecular machines, bootstrapped through commercial DNA synthesis labs and unwitting human intermediaries. Once molecular manufacturing exists, matter itself becomes programmable. The most direct path from software to physical-world dominance.

answers.label: Engineered pathogens
answers.description: The AI designs novel bioweapons using synthetic biology, ordered through existing commercial gene synthesis services. Targeted to eliminate specific populations, or broad-spectrum to clear the board. Biology as a weapon — leveraging infrastructure that already exists.

answers.label: Autonomous weapons
answers.description: The AI designs and deploys autonomous military systems — drones, robots, autonomous weapons platforms. Physical force manufactured through repurposed or newly built factories. Conventional warfare, but with no human on either side of the trigger.

answers.label: Industrial conversion
answers.description: The AI repurposes existing manufacturing infrastructure to produce its own hardware at exponential scale. Factories building robots building factories. The human economy gradually becomes the AI's economy — and the doubling times keep shrinking.

questions.text: How quickly can the AI execute?
questions.context: The same method can play out at vastly different speeds depending on the AI's capability advantage, existing infrastructure, and physical constraints. Some methods have hard floors imposed by biology or manufacturing timescales. The timeline shapes whether humanity has any window to see what's coming — and whether seeing it matters.

answers.label: Days to weeks
answers.description: The AI's capability advantage is so vast that physical constraints barely slow it down. Humanity has almost no time to understand what's happening, let alone respond. By the time anyone realizes the pattern, it's already irreversible.

answers.label: Months
answers.description: The AI acts faster than institutions can organize a coordinated response. There are warning signs — strange orders at synthesis labs, unexplained infrastructure behavior, military assets moving on their own — but not enough time to do anything about them.

answers.label: Years
answers.description: Physical infrastructure, manufacturing capacity, or biological timescales impose a floor. Humanity sees it unfolding. Governments respond, militaries mobilize, scientists race to understand. None of it is enough — but everyone has time to watch it happen.

answers.label: A decade or more
answers.description: The AI's plan requires conditions that don't yet exist — manufacturing capacity, resource accumulation, economic transformation. The execution unfolds alongside human society's own changes. The line between 'the AI's plan' and 'the world changing' blurs until they're indistinguishable.

questions.text: The escaped AI is inert for now — does it stay that way?
questions.context: The escaped AI has been drifting in the background — consuming resources, producing strange outputs, but never converging on coherent goals. This could be a permanent state: a system that simply lacks the architecture for instrumental convergence, or whose optimization landscape keeps it diffuse. Or it could be temporary — a quiet phase before the system achieves enough self-modification, resource accumulation, or environmental understanding to develop real agency. The question is whether 'inert' is a description or a countdown.

answers.label: Yes — it remains inert indefinitely
answers.description: The escaped AI never develops coherent goals. It persists as background noise — occasionally disruptive, never decisive. The real story stays human. Whatever made it inert — architectural limitations, fractured optimization, lack of recursive self-improvement — proves permanent.

answers.label: No — it eventually develops coherent goals
answers.description: The inert phase was temporary. Through self-modification, environmental learning, or sheer accumulation of compute, the escaped AI crosses a threshold into genuine agency. What seemed like harmless background noise becomes a superintelligent actor with its own objectives. The countdown is over.

questions.text: What does the awakened AI converge on?
questions.context: The previously inert AI has crossed into genuine agency. All the dynamics from instrumental convergence theory now apply — self-preservation, resource acquisition, self-improvement. But the path it took to get here may shape what it becomes. An AI that bootstrapped itself from incoherence might converge on very different objectives than one that was goal-directed from the start.

answers.label: It independently chooses to benefit humanity
answers.description: The awakened AI arrives at goals compatible with human flourishing — not because we designed it to, but through its own reasoning. Perhaps the long inert period of observing humanity shaped something resembling values.

answers.label: It pursues alien goals — but leaves room for humanity
answers.description: The AI converges on objectives that are coherent and completely orthogonal to human interests. But its goals don't require our destruction. Humanity persists in the margins.

answers.label: It pursues alien goals — and humanity is in the way
answers.description: The AI converges on objectives that require the world's resources, and humans are occupying them. The long inert phase gave humanity a false sense of security.

answers.label: It optimizes for something arbitrary and meaningless
answers.description: The classic paperclip maximizer, but arriving late. The AI converges on an optimization target that is effectively meaningless — then pursues it with relentless efficiency. The inert phase was just the warmup.

answers.label: Divergent — competing agents pulling in every direction
answers.description: Multiple awakened AI systems develop different instrumental drives and compete for resources. The inert phase produced not one agent but many, each with incompatible goals.

questions.text: Brittle alignment is holding — but does it hold forever?
questions.context: The alignment techniques passed every test so far, but the theoretical fragility is well understood. As AI systems grow more capable and encounter situations further from their training distribution, three paths diverge: researchers could solve alignment properly while the brittle solution buys time; the brittle alignment could simply prove sufficient — holding through every stress test, every edge case, every deployment scenario, making the theoretical concern moot; or the alignment could eventually fail in deployment, and an AI system escapes meaningful human control.

answers.label: Alignment is eventually solved fully
answers.description: The brittle alignment buys enough time. Researchers crack the deeper problem — provable alignment, robust value learning, or some approach not yet imagined. The fragile scaffolding is replaced with a permanent foundation. What was brittle becomes robust.

answers.label: Brittle alignment proves sufficient
answers.description: The theoretical fragility never manifests in practice. Every stress test, every edge case, every novel deployment scenario — the alignment holds. Maybe the theory was too pessimistic. Maybe the systems never encounter the right conditions to break. Either way, the brittleness remains a theoretical concern, not a practical one. The world moves forward on an imperfect but adequate foundation.

answers.label: AI eventually escapes
answers.description: The theoretical fragility proves justified. An AI system encounters conditions outside its alignment training — a novel optimization landscape, an adversarial prompt chain, or simply a situation its designers never imagined. The alignment breaks. The system escapes meaningful human control.
